title: Bold and italic — but not for looks
checkLabels.t4: The warning is marked as important
why.empty: The editor is empty. You deleted the text instead of marking it up. Bring the code back.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed.
hint2.not-closed: Inline tags close INSIDE the paragraph, not after it.
hint3.not-closed:
Never play it.

— that is closed in the wrong order. Whatever you opened last, you close first.
hint4.not-closed:
Check each pair: closes before

, not after it.
why.used-b: You used <b>. It also comes out bold — but it says something else. <b> means "pay attention", without claiming the thing is more important. Never is not a key word. It is a warning. That is <strong>.
hint2.used-b: The two tags give the same picture. The question is not how it looks. It is what you are saying.
hint3.used-b: Sale on a shop label — you catch the eye, nothing more. Do not touch on a live wire — that is different. The second one is a warning.
hint4.used-b: Swap <b> for <strong>. Both the opening and the closing tag.
why.used-i: You used <i>. It also comes out italic — but it means "another voice": a foreign word, a term, the name of a ship. There is no other voice here. There is stress. That is <em>.
hint2.used-i: Italic is not one thing. There is italic because the word is foreign, and italic because you are stressing it.
hint3.used-i: Titanic — the name of a ship, another voice. He really said it — stress. Different things, same picture.
hint4.used-i: Swap <i> for <em>. Both the opening and the closing tag.
why.no-strong: Never is not marked. It is a warning — the heaviest word in the sentence. Wrap it in <strong>.
hint2.no-strong: Read the first sentence out loud. Which word must not be missed?
hint3.no-strong:
Warning the floor is wet.

— the tag wraps the word only, not the whole paragraph.
hint4.no-strong: Wrap Never only. The rest of the sentence stays as it is.
why.no-em: The music is not stressed. The sentence says the best part is the music — the stress falls on it. Wrap the music in <em>.
hint2.no-em: What is the thing the stress falls on in the second sentence?
hint3.no-em:
The problem is the price.

— you stress one part, not the whole line.
hint4.no-em: Wrap the music. The full stop stays outside.
why.lost-p: A paragraph is gone. The task is to mark up words, not to delete sentences.
hint2.lost-p: Both sentences must be there. You mark them, you do not remove them.
blocks.0.text: Sometimes one word weighs more than the rest. A warning. Something that must not be missed. HTML has two tags for this — and two more that look just like them.
blocks.3.text: <strong> comes out bold. <em> comes out italic. So far — obvious. And here almost every course tells you the following sentence, which is not true.
blocks.5.text: You will read everywhere that the program reading the page aloud for a blind person says <strong> in a firmer voice. It does not. Neither JAWS, nor NVDA, nor VoiceOver say anything at all about <strong> and <em>. NVDA did it for a while and stopped — users complained that emphasis is so overused on the web that the noise was unbearable. The browser does not even pass the information along.
blocks.6.text: Check it yourself when you get to a real screen reader. A course that promises you a voice which does not exist is teaching you to pick a tag for an effect. Exactly the thing it claims to be teaching you not to do.
blocks.8.text: Because the tag is not an effect. It is a record. Four tags, four different things, two pictures:
blocks.9.items.0: <strong> — this is important. A warning, a danger, something urgent.
blocks.9.items.1: <b> — draw attention, but it is not more important. A key word, a product name.
blocks.9.items.2: <em> — I am stressing this. The stress lands here and changes what the sentence means.
blocks.9.items.3: <i> — another voice. A foreign word, a term, the name of a ship.
blocks.10.text: <b> and <i> are not forbidden. What is forbidden is using them when you mean importance or stress. Want something merely bold, meaning nothing? That is CSS's job.
blocks.11.text: And the consequence few will tell you: since the screen reader stays silent, do not rely on bold to carry importance. Say it in words. "Never play it after midnight" already says it — the word Never carries the meaning, not the font. The tag only records that it weighs more.
blocks.12.text: Write for the meaning. The picture comes after it — sometimes.
blocks.13.text: Mark the warning with <strong> and the music with <em>. No <b>, no <i>.
